issue_title: Growth, Water Scarcity, and Long-Term Supply in Nevada
purpose: A political-reality map for citizen deliberation: what was tried, what moved, what stalled, who showed up. No recommendations; history baskets describe the record, not what anyone should do.

basket_definitions.often_moved: Often moved before: in this dataset, similar ideas often became law or cleared both chambers.
basket_definitions.unfinished: Got support but didn't finish: majority floor votes, both-chamber passage, or strong momentum without enactment; timing and process deaths count.
basket_definitions.rarely_moved: Rarely moved before: repeated early stops (especially origin committee) for this kind of idea.
basket_definitions.mixed: The record splits: some versions of the idea moved, others repeatedly stalled.

session_snapshot.note: Highest enactment share of the four sessions (13 of 20).
Every 2021 failure in this set stopped in its origin committee. Two joint resolutions (81:AJR2, 81:AJR3) passed both chambers and were enrolled.
The only session where a bill (82:SB180) cleared both chambers unanimously and still was not enacted. 82:SJR3 (Colorado River resolution) was enrolled.
Largest bill count and largest failure count in the set; 10 of 16 failures stopped in origin committee.

theme_scorecards.theme: Who gets water and how permits are decided

examples.plain_topic: Streamlined hearing requirements for temporary changes to existing water rights
Temporary water-right changes up to 3 years for renewable energy projects
State-chartered water banks (Nevada Water Banking Act)
Monitoring-and-mitigation plans with public hearings before permit decisions

theme_scorecards.facts: 5 of 13 enacted. Enacted bills were narrow or procedural (deadlines, notices, hearing rules, small exemptions) and passed with near-unanimous floor votes. Bills that reshaped who decides or added new structures (water banks 81:AB354, hydrological-study fund 83:AB190, review-committee timelines 83:AB265) stopped in origin committee.
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: in this record, narrow procedural permit changes often moved, while structural changes to permit decision-making rarely cleared the first committee.

theme_scorecards.theme: Rules for underground water and wells
theme_scorecards.typical_stop_stage_when_not_enacted: origin_committee (4 of 8 failures); one bill (82:SB180) passed both chambers unanimously and still died

examples.plain_topic: Account to buy and permanently retire water rights in over-appropriated basins
Rules for ordering pumping cutbacks in over-drawn basins
How groundwater boards are established and dissolved
Managing groundwater and surface water together (conjunctive management)

theme_scorecards.facts: 7 of 15 enacted, the largest theme in the set. Enactments include substantive basin-management tools (80:AB95 pumping cutbacks, 80:SB140 10% groundwater reserve, 83:SB36 water-right retirement account). Water-right retirement failed in 2023 (82:SB176, after origin committee) and then passed unanimously in 2025 (83:SB36). Groundwater-board restructuring failed three times (81:SB149, 82:SB180, 83:AB363), once after clearing both chambers 41-0 and 21-0.
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: this theme moved more than most, and at least one idea (retiring water rights) went from stalled to enacted across sessions; groundwater-board restructuring is the recurring non-finisher.

theme_scorecards.typical_stop_stage_when_not_enacted: origin_committee (2 of 3 failures); one unanimous Senate bill died in the second chamber

examples.plain_topic: Ban on Colorado River water for decorative (nonfunctional) grass in southern Nevada
More water suppliers required to adopt conservation plans and water-loss audits
Interim committee review of conservation plus a state artificial-turf study
Restaurants serve drinking water only on request

theme_scorecards.facts: 3 of 8 enacted, plus two enrolled 2021 resolutions (81:AJR2, 81:AJR3). The enacted bills bind utilities and suppliers (audits, plans, the decorative-grass ban). 83:SB143 passed the Senate 21-0 and died in the Assembly.
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: supplier-facing conservation mandates repeatedly became law in this record; the small sample limits conclusions about consumer-facing rules, which appear once (82:AB186) and stopped early.

theme_scorecards.theme: Planning for growth and future water supply

examples.plain_topic: Counties and cities must keep long-term water resource plans with their master plans
When governments can require water-right dedication before approving development
State economic development plan must address efficient water use
Advisory Board on Water Resources Planning and Drought Resiliency

theme_scorecards.facts: 5 of 8 enacted; every enactment passed its best floor vote unanimously. Failures were a study mandate (80:AB265), a new advisory board (80:SB499), and a building-code preemption (83:AB385). Note: 3 of the 8 bills are weak title matches, so the strong-title record is 3 of 5 enacted.
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: planning requirements tied to existing local processes often moved in this record; proposals creating new advisory bodies or studies stopped early.

theme_scorecards.theme: Colorado River and deals with other states

examples.plain_topic: Updated California-Nevada law-enforcement compact for Lake Tahoe and Topaz Lake
Resolution urging the Bureau of Reclamation to protect and manage the Colorado River
Rework the Colorado River Commission's water and power role
Change Colorado River Commission membership

theme_scorecards.facts: Only 4 bills. Both attempts to restructure the Colorado River Commission (80:SB76, 81:AB15) died in origin committee. The compact update and the resolution passed unanimously.
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: sample is too small for strong conclusions; the only visible pattern is that both Commission-restructuring attempts stopped at the first committee.

theme_scorecards.theme: Money for water projects and agencies

examples.plain_topic: Tribal governments eligible for river channel clearance and maintenance grants
Money for a Division of Water Resources study of extreme rainfall events
Grant money for cities and counties to develop water resource plans
Money to a UNLV lab for water systems and wastewater work

theme_scorecards.facts: 11 of 13 enacted, the highest rate of any theme; every enacted bill passed its best floor vote at 95% yes or higher. The two failures were grant/appropriation asks that never left origin committee (82:SB102, 83:SB159).
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: in this record, funding bills that reached a floor vote passed overwhelmingly; the filter was the money committee, not the floor.

theme_scorecards.typical_stop_stage_when_not_enacted: origin_committee (3 of 5 failures); 2 died in the second chamber after majority floor votes

examples.plain_topic: Health districts may pay 100% of septic-to-sewer conversion costs
State plans to control non-point-source water pollution
Legionnaires' disease and building water systems
Ban small disposable plastic water bottles near the Lake Tahoe watershed

theme_scorecards.facts: 3 of 8 enacted. This theme has the lowest median winning floor margin (80%) of any theme with multiple votes — floor votes here were contested more often than elsewhere. Two bills won majority floor votes and still died in the second chamber (82:AB263, 83:SB324).
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: this theme splits between early committee stops and second-chamber deaths; it holds more of the set's contested floor votes than any other theme.

theme_scorecards.theme: Who referees water disputes (courts and the State Engineer's office)
theme_scorecards.typical_stop_stage_when_not_enacted: origin_committee (3 of 6); 2 recent bills got majority floor votes then died in the second chamber

examples.plain_topic: Constitutional amendment sending State Engineer challenges to the Court of Appeals
Require (not just encourage) best available science; recognize groundwater-surface water connections
Deadlines and procedures for adjudicating old 'vested' water-right claims
Biennial public reports on water applications pending 2+ years

theme_scorecards.facts: 0 of 6 enacted — the only theme with no enactments across all four sessions. Three died in origin committee (2021 bills); two more recent bills (82:AB387, 83:SB31) won majority floor votes in one chamber and died in the other.
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: in this record, changing who reviews the State Engineer never finished, though the stopping point moved later in recent sessions (from origin committee in 2021 to second-chamber deaths in 2023 and 2025). Six bills is enough to note the pattern but not to call it fixed.

theme_scorecards.relevance_note: Low relevance for water deliberation: 10 of 13 bills are weak title matches (boats, boilers, highway names, food regulation). Their high enactment rate says little about water policy.

examples.plain_topic: Voting and office-holding rights in irrigation districts for land held in trust
Food-establishment regulation, including water standards, moved to Department of Agriculture
State Engineer shielded from liability for certain dam or reservoir failures

theme_scorecards.facts: 10 of 13 enacted, but only 3 bills are strong water-title matches. This theme exists because the keyword search caught them; it is background, not a water-policy pattern.
theme_scorecards.inference: Inference: no water-specific conclusion is warranted from this catch-all theme.

people_and_process_signals.note: Descriptive only. No ranking of legislators as good or bad.

who_carried_bills.name: Pete Goicoechea
who_carried_bills.description: Appeared most often as a primary sponsor in this set (13 bills across 2019-2023), heavily on groundwater/wells and planning bills; his sponsored bills include both enactments (80:SB150, 80:SB250, 82:SB113) and committee stalls (82:SB102, 82:SB176).

who_carried_bills.name: Howard Watts
who_carried_bills.description: Appeared on conservation and clean-water bills across all four sessions, including enacted 80:AB163 (water-loss audits, WaterSense fixtures) and 83:SB276.

who_carried_bills.name: Rich DeLong
who_carried_bills.description: Appeared on 2023-2025 bills, including enacted conservation-plan expansion 82:AB191 and several 2025 State Engineer process bills that stalled (83:AB265, 83:AB312, 83:AB419).

who_carried_bills.name: Bert Gurr
who_carried_bills.description: Appeared on permit-process bills (82:AB90, 83:AB190, 83:AB265, 83:AB419); all four stopped in or just after origin committee.

who_carried_bills.name: Chris Brooks
who_carried_bills.description: Appeared on 2019 bills including enacted conservation bill 80:AB163 and well-replacement bill 80:SB236.

who_carried_bills.name: James Settelmeyer
who_carried_bills.description: Appeared on 2019-2021 planning and district bills; all four (80:SB150, 80:SB232, 80:SB250, 81:SB98) were enacted.

committee_sponsorship.fact: About half the set was introduced by committees (mostly Natural Resources and money committees) rather than individual legislators.

cross_party_sponsorship.fact: Only 4 of 43 person-sponsored bills list both major parties among person sponsors.

examples.theme: Rules for underground water and wells
Planning for growth and future water supply
Rules for underground water and wells

cross_party_sponsorship.inference: Inference: formal cross-party co-sponsorship was rare in this set; 3 of the 4 cross-party bills were enacted, but 4 bills is too few to generalize.

committee_choke_points.fact: 26 of 40 failures (65%) stopped in the bill's origin committee, split almost evenly between chambers (14 Assembly, 12 Senate). The pack records the stage a bill stopped at, not which named committee held it, and most committee meetings in the underlying data have no recorded roll call, so committee-level attribution is not supported.

high_support_non_enactments.definition: Bills or resolutions that won at least one majority-Yes final-passage floor vote but did not become law. Possible timing/process unfinished business — a majority vote in one chamber does not prove statewide popularity or that the idea would pass again.

deliberation_prompts: If an idea often stalled in the first committee — like water banking (81:AB354) or Colorado River Commission changes (80:SB76, 81:AB15) — what would have to change for it to be realistic next time?
82:SB180 passed both chambers 41-0 and 21-0 and still never became law. Is that a 'no' on the idea of restructuring groundwater boards — or a process problem worth distinguishing from opposition?
Retiring water rights failed in 2023 (82:SB176) and passed unanimously in 2025 (83:SB36). What changed between sessions, and does that history apply to other stalled ideas?

Funding bills that reached a floor vote passed at 95-100% yes, but two funding asks (82:SB102, 83:SB159) never left committee. When the filter is the money committee rather than the floor, what does 'support' mean?

Every bill in this set about who reviews the State Engineer (6 bills, 2021-2025) failed, but the two most recent ones (82:AB387, 83:SB31) got majority floor votes before dying in the second chamber. Is that trajectory evidence of growing traction, or just noise from a small sample?

Only 4 of 43 person-sponsored bills had sponsors from both major parties. Does a group's idea need cross-party sponsors to fit the pattern of what passed here, given that most enactments were committee-sponsored or near-unanimous anyway?
2025 introduced the most water bills (26) and failed the most (16, ten in origin committee). Does a crowded session change how a group would time or bundle an idea?
Clean-water bills had the most contested floor votes (median winning margin 80% vs. 100% elsewhere). When an idea passes but with real opposition, what does that mean for how durable the policy is?

The pack cannot say why any bill stalled — no fiscal notes, testimony, or lobbying records. Which of these patterns would a group want to check against people who were in the room before relying on them?

certainty_notes.pattern: Funding bills often moved (11 of 13 enacted)
Groundwater/wells theme moved more than most but with recurring board-restructuring failures
Narrow permit-process changes moved; structural permit changes stalled early
State Engineer oversight bills never finished
Planning mandates tied to existing local processes often moved
Clean-water bills split between early stops and second-chamber deaths
Colorado River Commission restructuring stopped at origin committee
certainty_notes.note: Two examples only; insufficient for strong conclusions.
Single example (82:AB186); insufficient for strong conclusions.
Four examples only; insufficient for strong conclusions.

data_limits_carried_forward: Keyword-discovered set of 88 bills; not a proven complete universe of water-relevant legislation.
18 of 88 bills are weak title matches; theme 9 ('Other bills that touch water') is mostly noise for water deliberation.
Single data source (NELIS); OpenStates lookups returned empty.
Stage labels record where a bill stopped, not why; no fiscal notes, testimony, or lobbying records exist in the pack.
Most committee meetings lack recorded roll calls, so committee choke points can only be described at the stage level, not by named committee.
The three 'In Progress' items are enrolled joint resolutions that do not become statutes.
Amendment-impact comparisons exist only for the 48 Governor-bound bills.
handoff: Reality Mapper finished. Run Citizen Brief Writer (and Appendix Builder can run in parallel).
